Context setting can't be migrated into non-context because migrations are done with two separate (class) methods. As I recall, I've once done this; the trick was to *not* migrate the setting, but keep it in context settings and store it to non-context after calling `openContext`. But this obviously works only if you keep some context settings, and only migrate some of them to non-context.

In this case, I got rid of context settings altogether (because all settings in the bottom part would be non-context, so it is better to simply keep the only "context" setting, the variable name, as "hint" - the practice we already have in many widgets), so the above trick can't work. Still a messy regex, but good enough.

When doing regexes, I try to always use named groups where appropriate. 
On the one hand, the regex becomes more readable as the "purpose" of a capturing group is clarified in the regex
On the other hand you can use the names later when pulling stuff out of the group object, which makes the code much clearer.
E.g., you can test with `if match.group("number") is None...`, etc.

I came up with this, but haven't run the tests:
```
r"^\s*(?:(?P<name>C|CH|CHAN|CHANNEL)\s*(?P<number>\d))|(?P<name_only>MATH|LINE)\s*$"
```
You can then (I think) simplify the logic to "if a number is found, use C + number, else use name_only"

You can try this out if you want.

> Could we disable this lint rule and restore these refactors?

We don't _have_ to have this lint rule enabled 😆. However, on reading large amounts of code and performing this refactor (cibuildwheel, plumbum, boost-histogram, and partially awkward), I find it to be very helpful for nested structures, and a tradeoff for shallow ones; and even then, it sometimes prompts real improvements, like moving the "return" clauses to the top. It reduces unnessiary whitespace, and it avoids dangling "else"'s that that are hard to match up with if's.

The key thing for me is you _can't_ ignore the body of the if - they have return's in them, and returns _are_ control flow, so the "else" is completely redundant and can be removed. Since it _can_ be removed, you have three choices: alway include it (terrible, would add tons of whitespace), always disallow it (this check), or mix and match and force the reader to have to digest both.

While when you write it, you might have an intent you are trying to convey, but there is no code impact at all; it is transparent to Python, so it really doesn't help the reader. It also creates long elif chains that are mentally taxing, while you can look at each guard clause in isolation; they are just "narrowing" (and this is exactly how MyPy works, actually).

## `goreleaser` deps

> But are they mandatory to be downloaded just for the purpose of running `goreleaser`?

Yes, as you found out by running `go get github.com/goreleaser/goreleaser@latest` (or `go mod tidy` etc), they're all necessary for `goreleaser` and only `goreleaser`.

### Go modules

> In fact, I started coding Go as a casual coder transposing my experience from other language. But at the time I started Go module was not made that way or not at all.

Yea Go modules didn't exist I think until beta releases of Go `1.11` IIRC. Before that, there was no single dependency management system in Go. Now Go modules standard and built-in, thankfully.

I don't write that much Go myself, so it seemed like you might know the ecosystem better than me at first haha 😅  
In particular, Go workspaces themselves were more complicated than Go modules. So if you were familiar with workspaces, I thought you might have seen modules by now.

> So yes I'm not so familiar with module. I will learn.

No worries though! Go modules are pretty straightforward and simpler than workspaces, so it seems like you've mostly figured it out already!

### tool dependency

> But I suppose, that they're probably not needed to be listed as part of the project? I suppose `goreleaser` can be installed outside. As a tool, does it have to be registered as to be like "part" of the project? It's an helper, right?
> 
> I mean, I don't list the lib used to build `make` as part of the project.

So it is a tool dependency, that's why I put it in [`tools.go`](https://github.com/docopt/docopts/pull/65/files#diff-ce07b69075d5f4237197bb90b721f2b357c8567a1a8945068239b32e57d85552R8).

In other languages, such as JavaScript, [`devDependencies`](https://docs.npmjs.com/specifying-dependencies-and-devdependencies-in-a-package-json-file) can be listed separately in the package manifest. In JS, `devDependencies` are only installed when you're working on the package itself. If you're just using the package as a dependency, e.g. `import jsreleaser from "jsreleaser";`, the `devDependencies` won't be installed, only `dependencies` will be.

Go modules do not seem to have a "formal" separation of these yet. Per [the Go issue I linked](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/25922#issuecomment-1038394599) above, `tools.go` is a current convention to list tool dependencies that aren't needed when importing, similar to `devDependencies` in JS.

JS's `devDependencies` also make it into JS's `package-lock.json` lockfile, similar to the `go.sum`.
So having `goreleaser` in `go.mod` and `go.sum` follows Go's current conventions. This ensures that anyone developing `docopts` -- in this case you and I -- has the same version of all dependencies, including indirect development dependencies.

#### `go.mod` nuances

As you found out, if you run `go get github.com/goreleaser/goreleaser@latest`, it'll automatically be added to the `go.mod` as an `// indirect` dependency. So it's meant to be listed there. Even if you remove it, it'll get added back as soon as it's installed.

As I was building the Homebrew package, I realized you can actually just run `go build` _without_ running `go mod tidy` first. And then it _only_ detects that `docopt-go` is a dependency (as it's the only dep in `docopts.go`) and _only_ installs it.

So I think I can remove the `go mod tidy` statement from the `README.md` for individual builds and leave it only for releases.

   should either be processed as regular RFC2136 updates, including

From there we could implement a small caching decorator using a WeakValueDict:
```python
def weak_cache(function):
  cache = WeakValueDictionary()
  def wrapper(*args):
    try:
      return cache[args]
    else:
      res = function(*args)
      cache[args] = res
      return res
  return wrapper
```
so that we could write
```python
@weak_cache

```

I've gone back and forth on if we should enumerate all the supported versions here or not..

--

This question speaks to a related check that we're going to need to add: in `pants_loader.py` checking for a valid interpreter. This was suggested first by Kris here https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/6062#issuecomment-401983486. It will assert that they're using a supported Py version, i.e. not Py2.6 or 3.0-3.5 (soon 3.6+). 

The reason this belongs in `pants_loader.py` and not here in `global_options.py` or `options_initializer.py` is that we cannot make any assumptions about them using this option and how they're setting the interpreter to run Pants with. Even if they completely circumvent this option / our setup repo and try running Pants with something like Py3.4, we need to run the assertion.

--

I think we probably want to avoid, then, enumerating the options here, as that's the job of `pants_loader.py`.

Instead, my original intent was to give an example of what this value is supposed to look like, i.e. that it should be the major and minor number like `2.7` or `3.6`. We need it to be like this because of the check here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/7363/files#diff-c30fdc263a0a6d8522b90488e2a264d5R112.

This is good but a bit brittle and possibly over-elaborate. You have to manually create `FakeWorkUnit` instances, and keep them in sync with your expected struct.  

You can make this test simpler and yet more comprehensive, by iterating over some workunit struct (made out of dicts), creating FakeWorkUnits and generating callbacks on the fly, and then checking that you get the *same* struct back! Then you can try this on several different structs, and you don't have to keep the input and the expected output in sync.

Something like this:

```
def make_fake_workunit(wu_dict, parent_wu):
  return FakeWorkUnit(...) # Set up what you need from wu_dict and parent_wu.

def generate_callbacks(wu_dict, parent_wu=None):
  wu = make_fake_workunit(wu_dict, parent_wu)
  reporter.start_workunit(fake_wu)
  for child_dict in wu_dict.children:
    generate_callbacks(child_dict, wu_dict)
  reporter.end_workunit(fake_wu)

def check_callbacks(root_wu_dict):
  generated_wu_dict = generate_callbacks(root_wu_dict)
  self.assertDictEqual(root_wu_dict, generated_wu_dict)
```

I'm sure I got a couple of details wrong there, but does this make sense in general? 

Then it's easy to call `check_callbacks` on a variety of different structs, to test various edge cases and so on.

What about refactoring to check these sequentially:
```
# 1. Check input consistency
- Check that at least a pair of inputs is given 
- Check that not too types of inputs are given at the same time
- If wavenum or wavelengh, or wmin is dimensionned, check that wunit is consistent (if not default)
- [we can maybe leave the check that Medium is given in the wavelength case for later]

# 2. Conversion to base units (strip dimensioned units, convert to nm or cm )
# Now no need to assert / raise anything. 
wmin, wmax given
 --> if not dimensioned: convert to nm or cm-1 based on wunit  (no need to assert, we did it in 1.). Then write wavelength_min/max , or wavenum_min/max
 --> if dimensioned, convert to nm, or cm-1. Then write wavelength_min/max, or wavenum_min/max
wavenum_min, wavenum_max given
 --> convert to cm-1
wavelength_min, wavelength_max given:
--> convert to nm

# now we only have wavenum_min/max, or wavelen_min/max, in standard units (cm-1, or nm). Last thing to check: converting nm > cm-1 depends on the propagation medium (air/vacuum)
# 3. Convert to cm-1 
if wavenum_min/max: 
--> nothing to do 
if wavelength_min/max
--> check that medium is given
--> convert to cm-1 with nm2cm or nm_air2cm  (handy function in convert.py) 

Return wavenum_min/max

# Reference is https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Basics_of_HTTP/MIME_types
# Where we can see MIME type can have optional parameter
# We can have a space or not in charset
def is_json_mimetype(mimetype):
    """
    :type mimetype: str
    :rtype: bool
    """
    simple_mime_type, optional_parameter = mimetype.split(";") if \
        ";" in mimetype else \
        (mimetype, None)
    maintype, subtype = simple_mime_type.split('/')  # type: str, str

    return maintype == 'application' and \
           (
                   subtype == 'json' or
                   subtype.endswith('+json')
           ) and \
           (
                   optional_parameter is None or
                   optional_parameter.strip() == "charset=utf-8"
           )

I believe I'm not explaining it that well. Basically, NT does not allow you to delete a file which is currently being used by a process (used also means opened for read-only, etc). Not only that, but NT does not allow the same process to delete the file without first explicitly closing it. Therefore, the `close()` method must be first invoked - this happens automatically at the end of the with-statement. Now, the `remove()` method can only be called after the `close()` method, so it must be outside the context manager. Hope this explains it.

> I see. I think the disagreement here stems from viewing this as a post-condition. In my mind, the goal is to mimic for first-class assets the guarantees that any blockchain provides for its native token: maximum transfer guarantees at the wire format level -- not a general purpose ability to provide post-conditions (which is where things can get tricky, because we'll be fighting the urge to define a new domain-specific language for encoding post-conditions, and trying to draw lines on what kinds of data may be inputs to the post-conditions). If all we're doing is providing maximum transfer guarantees, then a lot of those design choices become much simpler.

I think I have my answer to my original question -- the purpose of the `log_*()` methods and the `AssetMap` are to prevent double-spending of assets.  That we could use this information to enforce post-conditions is a serendipitous side-effect, but not the intended goal of these methods and structs.

That said, I stand by my proposal -- I think there's more than enough evidence from the plethora of lost/stolen funds from smart contracts that users would greatly benefit from explicitly applying post-conditions on their accounts' assets.  I also think that the fact that this is still a problem today despite language-level support for post-conditions means that language-level support is not good enough -- post conditions must be a first-class transaction concern, and must be applied _outside of_ the language runtime.

To answer your immediate concerns, I'm willing to commit to _not_ implementing a DSL -- I am only concerned with making boolean statements about the quantity/presence of assets in the originating account (i.e. the account that sends the transaction; not necessarily the account that pays the transaction fee).  Each post-condition is a triple -- an asset name, a comparator, and a literal value to compare it to.  I don't care to support anything else beyond this, since the whole point of this feature as I understand it is to help the principal who sends the transaction remain safe from bad contracts.  I firmly believe that complex expressions on account assets, or statements about other accounts' assets, should be done within the Clarity code-body.

This entire comment feels like it belongs in a `.. note::` block rather than just free-floating. This docstring could benefit from adding a short description of _how_ clusters are matched. That might be a more useful way of presenting the discussion of `neighbors` vs `env_neighbors` currently in the class docstring. Here's a suggested starting point:

> An environment is defined by the bond vectors between a particle and its neighbors as defined by `env_neighbors`. For example, `env_neighbors={'num_neighbors': 8}` means that every particle's local neighborhood is defined by its eight nearest neighbors. Then, each particle's environment is compared to the environment of the other particles in the system. Which pairs of environments are compared is controlled by the `neighbors` parameter. For example, `neighbors={'r_max': 3.0}` means that the environment of each particle will be compared to the environment of every particle within a distance of 3.0.
>
> Two environments are compared using [point-set registration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Point-set_registration). Specifically, we use the [Kabsch algorithm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kabsch_algorithm) to find the optimal rotation matrix mapping one environment onto another. However, the Kabsch algorithm assumes a one-to-one correspondence between points in each environment, whereas we typically do not know which point in one environment corresponds to a particular point in another environment. The brute force solution here is to try all possible correspondences, but the resulting combinatorial explosion makes this problem infeasible, so we use a tolerance threshold to terminate the search when we have found a permutation of points that results in a sufficiently low RMSD.

> Is the `new PyModuleDef()` actually useful?

Good point; I don't really know. Things worked like that before, so I just kept it, but it's worth an investigation.

Is there any point to constructing a module without `PYBIND11_MODULE`? Nested submodules are created with `module_::def_submodule`, modules are imported through `module_::import`, and extra embedded modules are added through `PYBIND11_EMBEDDED_MODULE`. So I don't know if there a valid use case for constructing a loose, non-nested, standalone module which is not the top-level module of an extension?

If not, maybe the `module` constructors ought to just be private (providing access through `pybind11::detail::create_module`)?

If there is a use case and this is used, we might not want to break old code? Correct me if I'm wrong, but the `new` vs. `static` doesn't actually matter a lot, and is just to shut up the leak detectors? In practice, both a `static` `PyModuleDef`as well as a `new`-allocated one will just stay alive for as long as Python is running.
So I saw #2413 more as a way to make valgrind and other sanitizers happy (which is a perfectly valid thing to do; don't get me wrong! :-) ), rather than plugging an actual leak.

Yes. I expect so.  Sounds ok to me to allow one retx before overall timeout? What do you think?

- Default coap parm is `ack-timeout = 2 s` with `factor = 3/2 = 1.5`
- So initial wait is randomly selected in `[2, 3]` seconds.
- After than we double the retx/wait time 
  - So even if `2` is picked, first retx after `2`, second one would be at `2+2*2=6` which won't happen if we use overall timeout of `5` sec..

We can define different CoAP params for this, but not sure if we can have better values:
- One thing that I sort of not like about CoAP retx model, is the initial random interval that is picked will be reused for all next attempts (same value is doubled). 
- So a device picking shorter initial time will retx faster and finish earlier (unless we use `factor` very close to `1` which does not sound good).

> At the very least, let's call this field on TestResult python_sqlite_coverage_file or something similar to make it abundantly clear what it is (and to force anyone who consumes it to change their code when we change format).

But, right now, the field `TestResult.coverage_digest` is completely generalizable. It's up to the test runner implementation to decide what files they want to store that in. `pytest-cov` might store it as a `sqlite` file because that's how that tool works, whereas a JVM coverage tool might store it in XML because that's what's idiomatic to the tool.

The only thing that `rules/core/test.py` will do with the field is to collect all the generic `TestResult.coverage_digest` and then, through a union, convert that collection into one single `CoverageReport`. _The logic for merging is provided by the language_ based on what is idiomatic for that tool, not through some generic merge rule in `rules/core`.

> this change... will break the current code behaviour

As noted, I think it's good to investigate this.

From our own investigation...

I would not describe this as `breaking` any current behavior.

It does `change` the current behavior. 

But if we say the change `breaks` something... that sounds like: something will stop working.

So - would this `change` cause any currently working code to `break`? 

We do not think it will. 

To illustrate...

Say your CDK code looks like this:

```python
code = aws_lambda.DockerImageCode.from_image_asset(
    directory="/Users/john/PycharmProjects/project-root/application/runtime/",
    file="Dockerfile",
)

docker_lambda = aws_lambda.DockerImageFunction(code=code)
```

This works with the current code.

And this will still work after this PR is merged.

Currently - the SAM CLI only works with CDK code where `file` is just the `Dockerfile` and `directory` is the full path to the `Dockerfile`.

In contrast, CDK code like this...

```python
code = aws_lambda.DockerImageCode.from_image_asset(
    directory="/Users/john/PycharmProjects/project-root",
    file="./application/runtime/Dockerfile",
)

docker_lambda = aws_lambda.DockerImageFunction(code=code)
```

...does not currently work with the SAM CLI. But it will work when this PR is merged.

An important question would be – 

Why was the code written like this to begin with?

In the [PR where this code was introduced](https://github.com/aws/aws-sam-cli/pull/3492) – this does not seem to have been discussed in the comments. 

What this code behaviour specified in your internal tools e.g. Jira? If so, what were the reasons given?

The goal of this feature is to be compatible with the CDK.

But currently, the code is not supporting an important capability of the CDK.

Both `cdk deploy` and `sam build` work fine with metadata like:

```python
DockerContext ="/Users/john/PycharmProjects/project-root",
Dockerfile ="./application/runtime/Dockerfile",
```

So we ware not sure why code has been written to ensure sam always works with metadata like:

```python
DockerContext ="/Users/john/PycharmProjects/project-root/application/runtime",
Dockerfile ="Dockerfile",
```

Personally, I don't see this as a simplification. While using `||` is relatively common in JavaScript-land when setting default values, using `&&` in this way is not. While `||` lends itself to saying `this OR that`, permitting a mental model other than truth tables, `&&` translates to `this AND that` which people commonly (although [mistakenly](https://www.ecma-international.org/publications/files/ECMA-ST/Ecma-262.pdf)) assume will be a `boolean`.

Anecdotally, that this erroneous mental model is common is supported by the fact that I've both seen and experienced interviews in which one is, as something of a trick question to distinguish language "aficionados", asked the result type of `'' && null`.

And while Python behaves similarly to JavaScript,

```python
>>> '' and false
''
```

Julia, e.g., goes so far as to prohibit non-boolean values in boolean contexts.

```julia
julia> "" && false
ERROR: TypeError: non-boolean (String) used in boolean context
```

In short, I personally tend to favor being explicit rather than relying on language quirks that are neither widely ubiquitous (despite being shown in the React documentation) as convention nor straightforward to immediately reason about.

As far as I've been able to find, this `std::tie` unpacking (to the left of an assignment) is allowed in C++11, so it's fine.

There are a few places where I've used `std::pair` to return two values from a function, such as `minmax_depth`, `branch_depth`, `offsets_and_flatten`, and `nextcarry_outindex`. Those would be easier to read as a `std::tie`, but I didn't know C++ had this feature! Maybe some of these can adopt the `std::tie` technique: `nextcarry_outindex` is only ever used internally, so that's a good candidate. The others are mapped as Python return values by pybind11, so it depends on whether pybind11 recognizes the `std::tie` in the same way that it does `std::pair`. (It probably does, but I don't know for certain.)

The `stop` element of a slice isn't inclusive ever.  `Slice(0, 30)` is equivalent to `[0:30]` which returns 30 elements, the last one being index 29.  `_pos_to_idx()` on the `pos+ext` value returns the `last_index`, the index of the last value that should be included in the return slice, so the slice stop value should be `last_index + 1`.

Yeah, textx-lang-questionnaire is not yet deployed to PyPI, so once we get that uploaded it will be pulled in from there. Also, textX-dev is not yet deployed, so once it does you will be able to do `pip install textX[dev]` to pull in optional `dev` dependencies. 

That `[dev]` suffix is a setuptools way to specify dependencies that are optional and only needed if some particular feature is desired. That extra dependencies can be called however you like and if you want to install some of those you just use `[]` suffix with the names of optional deps separated by commas. I named these dependencies `test` and `dev` for obvious reasons :) This should be cleaner than using requirements file.

I think I had incorrectly assumed that being a singleton, that `NotImplemented` was treated like `None` and that you're allowed to supply it. In fact, mypy doesn't use or need it at all (https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/6710#issuecomment-485580032) and so it isn't often used. Given that comment, `Any` seems like the correct type to return.
Alternatively, we could combine the two signatures into one and simply say that the type of the second argument is always object, because it will always be accepted and mypy will assume that NotImplemented will be returned regardless. It's less useful for a user that way, but it resolves the original issue without much extra complexity in the implementation.

> The Target's snapshot can only contain files relevant to the target,

Agreed.

> so it should be possible to apply the globs captured on the CLI directly, which has the same effect as the intersection

Generally, yes, but the one foil is `FilesystemIgnoreSpec`. Those are not preserved in the `TargetAdaptorWithOrigin`, where `Origin` is only one single `FilesystemResolvedSpec`. Ignores apply to every single include glob, e.g. `./pants fmt2 '*.java' '*.py' '!test_*'`, so for us to have access to the `FilesystemIgnoreGlob`s passed, we would need to start storing that as a sequence in the `FilesystemResolvedSpec`.

Much simpler IMO is to preserve what literal files were resolved at the time that resolution happens:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/93c3567abeb7d19818767d3d20977e5e9ee15ca3/src/python/pants/engine/legacy/graph.py#L781-L789
 
With this design, a `FilesystemResolvedSpec` has a field `resolved_files` as a simple tuple of file names:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/93c3567abeb7d19818767d3d20977e5e9ee15ca3/src/python/pants/base/specs.py#L326-L331

--

Why can't we simply pass `origin.resolved_files` / why do we have to do this intersection? Globs, as explained in the comment. If the original spec is `*.py`, but the target only owns `test_strutil.py`, we need to ensure that we don't also try telling Pytest to run `test_dirutil.py`, `test_osutil.py`, etc.

Because the signature of the function itself is associated to the way the enhance logic works.

At high level this function, and the other functions related to enhance, are grouped in the dict `_enhance_func`. The effective function to call is accessed by index and then called with the parameters provided by `enhance()`. The signature of `enhance()` itself is controlled by the `Installer` interface, that `NginxConfigurator` implements, which dictates that `options`, the argument 2 passed to enhance functions, is of type `Optional[Union[List[str], str]]`.

I'm a little surprised by the way we're storing diffs.

I think that using the inputs like this imposes a very strong requirement that all changes are visible via the inputs--i.e. there are no input vs. state differences. This is different from the model used by providers, where diffs can be observed either from input <-> input or from input <-> state. An alternative approach would be to store the detailed diff as returned by the provider (polyfilling a detailed diff where necessary), then checking that the detailed diff from the update is constrained to the detailed diff from the preview, with old values sourced from the old inputs or old state as directed by the detailed diff.

I think that what is implemented might be okay assuming that in the vast majority of cases all input <-> state differences are essentially input <-> input differences b/c the differing property has the same value in the state and the inputs (though that assumption requires that the provider's `Read` method can accurately reflect values from the state back to the inputs).

I was also a little surprised that we're storing a flat list of top-level diffs rather than the tree diff (ala `resource.ValueDiff`). Is there a benefit to the flat list? Storing the tree diff might make error presentation a little more precise.

> Idea of the abc.ABC is - not let initialize classes without implementation, but give general interface for all child classes.

Well, the whole point of ABC's not being initializable if they contain abstract methods is that other concrete functions in that ABC may be relying on the abstract function which lacks implementation, and even `__init__` may be one of those functions. For that reason, these classes simply block intialization if that's the case.

Class that's just providing general interface for all child classes is called a base class, and it doesn't necessarily need to be abstract, in which case there's nothing that may cause issues when initializing, such as having methods without implementation getting called.

> But abstract dataclass - actually class without any provided logic, that must be added in children class, so why we should allow initialization of some abstract dataclasses?

With our case, these classes have no chance of "not working properly", since they're only really designed to be containers for some attributes (that's also why they're dataclasses), and there's no reason to prevent initialization since there's no possibility of accidentally calling an unimplemented method, since there aren't any actual abstract methods, they may be base classes, but they're not necessarily abstract base classes.

To be fair, some of these classes do in fact have abstract methods, making them abstract base (data)classes, and those should therefore inherit from `abc.ABC`, and have their initialization prevented. But for those classes which don't have any of these abstract methods, we don't need to do that.

Instead, for non-abstract base classes, the fact that they aren't necessarily something that will be used for anything meaningful should be mentioned in their docstring, and it should be somewhat obvious from their name (such as making the name start with `Base`, indicating that this is a base class, which will be inherited by other classes, but won't necessarily be used directly)

> They are just giving interface, and nothing else. Additional implementation must be added in children classes.

Yes, you can argue that the user shouldn't ever need to be initializing these, but that doesn't mean we absolutely have to literally prohibit their initialization. In some languages, this would indeed be the expected behavior, however python isn't one of these languages. By default, python doesn't really even support encapsulation, there's no such thing as a `private` or `public` function/class (there are some tricks to achieve that, but you're clearly not meant to do it). There are some conventions, such as the use of `_` prefix, indicating that this should only be used internally, but that's all it is, a convention.

Making something unaccessible isn't something we usually do in python, and if it's going to be done, there should be a really good reason for that, which in this case, there really isn't. Even though the class wasn't necessarily meant to be used like that, there is nothing that would technically go wrong if you initialized it, and there's isn't really the need for something to go wrong by us preventing it (unlike with abstract base classes, where initialization and further usage may lead to calls to unimplemented abstract methods).

if the user decided to initialize that class, just to store the things which are shared across both some bedrock and java server, why should we prevent him? We won't use that class anywhere directly, but that doesn't mean it literally shouldn't be usable.

Additionally, on top of this manual encapsulation being unpythonic, it's also quite complex and it may discourage some contributors, or people just looking over the code trying to understand how it works, and what's available.

I simply don't think we need this, and in my opinion, it just introduces more necessary maintenance, possible bugs, unpythonic behavior, needless complexity, and needless preventions for the user without any real benefit. I think it should simply be removed.

I'm not at all opposed to that if we find it being widely used (I think right now it's in two packages, this would be a third). The main issue is that it would either force those packages to have a hard dependency on a new zope.interface (which forces dropping 3.4) or they'd still have to have a copy of it for backwards compatibility.

> Maybe we should take it a step further and make `@Implementer` do that always (and have a lower-level decorator with a longer name do what `@Implementer` does today)?

That thought occurred to me, but I was very concerned that it would break backwards compatibility.. Right now, in non-strict-IRO mode, one can use a "redundant" `@implementer` declaration to forcibly change the `__iro__`, bringing an inherited interface from the back closer to the front. I don't know how widespread that might be, nor do I know how to attempt to evaluate it or slow-roll a deployment of it. (I haven't really given that last part much thought; it may be possible to do something where we default to strict IRO; if that fails, try implementer-if-needed mode, and if both fail go back to the legacy behaviour. That seems to complicate the test matrix though.)

See my other comment about pagination: the only way for the client to tell if it's reached the end of the data set is to keep requesting until it receives an empty list in the response. Since almost all of these mocks return only a single page of data, I simplified the mock so that if it receives a non-zero offset, it will return an empty response. So the first API request will not have an offset (or will have `offset=0`), and will return the mock data. The second API request will have `offset=50`, and will return an empty list. This allows the test to finish, rather than continually requesting pages and pages of data where every page is the same set of mock data, forever.

>Should we just try to do the string lookup in globals for the type when we end up with a string from inspect instead of eagerly running get_type_hints?

I think `globals()` would be insufficient because it returns the dict for the _current_ module namespace. We need access to the dict for the module namespace where the signature we're processing is written-- `get_type_hints` must access this under the hood somehow.

I think `try`-`except` block that catches `NameError` and falls back to the old behavior if `get_type_hints` fails is the way to go. Note that this will still make code like this (which crashes now) crash:

```
from __future__ import annotations

from dagster import op
from some_lib import SomeType

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING:
     from dagster import OpExecutionContext

@op
def foo(context: OpExecutionContext, bar: SomeType):
    ...
```

This will fail because, when we fall back to the old behavior, `SomeType` will no longer be dereferenced and it will be fed to Dagster machinery as a string.

I think we still should. Although not technically needed for the functionality, this would be a step back in terms of performance. Since unwrapping no longer happens by default, all operations inside the kernels retain the type. In turn that means that the default `__torch_function__` logic is called over and over again:

```python
import torch
from torchvision import datapoints
from torchvision.transforms.v2 import functional as F


class MyImage(datapoints.Image):
    @classmethod
    def __torch_function__(cls, *args, **kwargs):
        global num_calls
        num_calls += 1
        return super().__torch_function__(*args, **kwargs)


num_calls = 0

image = MyImage(torch.rand(3, 256, 256))


print(num_calls)

F.adjust_hue(image, hue_factor=0.25)

print(num_calls)
```

```
0
68
```

I picked `adjust_hue` as worst offender (I think?) here to make my point clear. On `main` the same snippet prints

```
0
0
```

```python
assert history[:, 'foo'] == [1, 2]
assert history[1:, 'foo'] == [2]
assert history[2:, 'foo'] == []  # Fails after PR 312
```

But I now realize that the behavior after PR 312 is more consistent when keys may not exist:
```python
history[:, 'bar']  # Raises KeyError
history[1:, 'bar']  # Raises KeyError
history[2:, 'bar']  # Raises KeyError after PR 312
```

Just to share some reading I've just done and how and why I have this somewhat inconsistent behavior as an author:

I don't like to put a period on a short, non-sentence phrase, e.g. `ctx: a Context object`
On a longer phrase like the above it becomes unclear to me whether or not it should be punctuated. It's a definition, not normal sentence, but it's longer and has more grammatical structure.

However! I'm fixing that behavior starting today. I just looked to a dictionary for guidance and found that OED puts a period after all definitions, short or long.
I'm tempted a little to follow that precedent even though I don't think it's the norm for programmers to put periods after very short phrases.

At the very least this removes, for me, any discomfort about punctuating a sentence fragment. I'll add periods here for sure, and I now have some new context to inform how I do this.

I'm still not sure how to handle very small phrases. OED puts a period on all definitions, no matter how short. Perhaps we should do the same, but it feels like an edge case...

OpenThread architecture defines a platform abstraction (a set of `otPlat<>` functions/definitions/APIs) which it delegates to platform layer to provide. Beyond the `otPlat<>` definitions, OpenThread stack itself does not need to be aware of how the platform implements these and what configurations and/or settings are used.

This PR (and earlier one) added  BLE platform definitions (`include/platform/ble.h`). OpenThread itself should interact with these definitions. Whether the underlying BLE platform implementation uses nimble or any other BLE stack should not really matter to OT code and how it would use BLE. That's my point about why such a configuration should be done at platform layer and not through openthread "configure.ac". 

Answering your question about precedent for this, some other platform implementations already use specific "platform-config.h" header file for adding config options specific to their need:
- Poisx-app uses `/src/posix/platform/platform-config.h`.
- Some radio driver examples also follow the same model: e.g.,`./examples/platforms/nrf52840/platform-config.h`

With regards to `--with-ncp-bus`, this config option is not changing/configuring the platform implementation. `ncp` is one of the executable outputs of OpenThread stack itself (we have `ncp` and `cli`). This option is defining which flavor of `ncp` to be built. That said, persoanlly I don't like the `--with-ncp-bus` option itself (would have preferred it to be added in a header file). I think the proposed improvement from  https://github.com/openthread/openthread/issues/3964 by @jwhui will help address/change this.

Ah, right. [Scikit-Learn's SGDClassifier][2] and [PyTorch's ReduceLROnPlateau][2] score after every `partial_fit` call, and stop training only if *all* of the most recent `patience` scores haven't improved by `tol`.

I implemented `patience_calls = int(self.patience) // 3` to balance between sampling after *every* `partial_fit` call and only after the *last* one. I did this because there is a larger cost to evaluating the validation set with Dask's parallel architecture.

`patience_calls = int(self.patience)` has clear documentation and provides the same benefit, though it might require a slightly higher `tol`. It provides clear performance benefits with Dask's parallel architecture.

[1]:https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/optim.html#torch.optim.lr_scheduler.ReduceLROnPlateau
[2]:https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier.html

I implemented it this way so stop-on-plateau can be layered on top of any existing adaptive algorithm with minimal developer work (i.e., so `_adapt` doesn't have to record plateau information). `_adapt` is only called when required by the underlying adaptive algorithm (i.e. Hyperband or successive halving).

`_to_reach` caches the value `_adapt` returns so plateaus can be evaluated more frequently than the number of `partial_fit` calls `_adapt` specifies. If `_adapt` specifies 10 `partial_fit` calls and `patience=3`, the estimator will need to be evaluated every `partial_fit` call to see if it's on a plateau, but `_adapt` should be not called until `partial_fit` has been called 10 times.

Here in MarkdownRenderer.render() we can pass in either a block token or a span token for rendering. If it's a block token, we'll look it up in the render_map and find the appropriate rendering method to call. All rendering methods for block tokens return a sequence of lines (without trailing endlines). If, on the other hand, it's a span token, then we call the self.span_to_lines method to render it into a sequence of lines (also without trailing endlines). The string join operation smacks an endline on each line before the join.

Why then don't the rendering methods already include the endlines? That's really just a matter of taste. I think it's cleaner to work with lines without the endlines and apply the endlines in a single place, that's all.

In the BaseRenderer, *all* the token rendering methods (block and span) return plain strings to be joined. Hence the render method can be simpler.

One could argue that this design where the overridden methods in a child class returns a different type of data from the base class isn't exactly a schoolbook example of inheritance. But I think the pros are bigger than the cons in this case and that it's actually pretty neat. The block tokens behave very differently from the span tokens from the perspective of the Markdown renderer. These rendering methods aren't supposed to be called by anyone outside the class. And python, being an untyped language, has no problem with it.

Wow, the changes in here are really elaborate. And these will be quite helpful once we decide to support `ivs` or `ivs-realtime`. But at this point in time I would suggest to actually exclude these services from the list of tested services in the `test_service_router` (see comment below), because:
- The service router is a very fundamental heuristic in LocalStack. It is already pretty complex, and we want to avoid adding complexity which isn't absolutely necessary.

Optimizing renders in a list with a "focused" / "selected" state that changes on hover / interaction is actually a classic scenario where you don't want to use vanilla React state since it will cause more components to re-render than the ones that changed. Couple that with our extremely light usage of `useMemo` and `useCallback` and it's a recipe for re-rendering everything.

The other way to solve this (this = not re-rendering the whole list and only re-render the item losing focus and the item gaining focus) is to use context like I did here:  https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/pull/10239

But that's uglier imo.

class AzureKeyCredentialPolicy(SansIOHTTPPolicy):
    """Adds a key header for the provided credential.

    :param credential: The credential used to authenticate requests.

    def __init__(
        self,
        credential: "AzureKeyCredential",
        name: str = "Authorization",
        auth_scheme: str = "",
        **kwargs  # pylint: disable=unused-argument
    ) -> None:
        super(AzureKeyCredentialPolicy, self).__init__()
        self._credential = credential
        if not name:

        if not isinstance(name, str):
            raise TypeError("name must be a string.")
        self._name = name
        self._auth_scheme = auth_scheme + " " if auth_scheme else ""

    def on_request(self, request):
        request.http_request.headers[self._name] = f"{self._auth_scheme}{self._credential.key}"

But lemel_ziv isn't neither the amount of fixed-length substrings nor the counter of fixed length substrings. The idea behind it is that the length of the substrings is chosen in a way to never have any substring occuring doubled.

Example: `100111`
First substring: just 1
Second: just 0
Third: would be 0 again, but we have 0 already so we need to choose 01
Forth: would be 1, but we have 1 already so we need to choose 11
End of the word -> 4 substrings / length 6 = 0.66.

The code is O(n) (the while loop needs approx n steps in in each we have a set check, which is O(1) and some other operations like addition, which are also O(1)), and I do not think we can be asymptotically better than this. There exist [cython](https://github.com/Naereen/Lempel-Ziv_Complexity/blob/master/src/lempel_ziv_complexity.pyx) implementations of that - but as long as those are not accessible easily via conda and pip, I do not see any nice way for us.

Here's a minimal example showing that the current 1D-array behavior can work in the Dask interface.

```python
from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs
import lightgbm as lgb
import numpy as np
import dask.array as da
from distributed import Client, LocalCluster

cluster = LocalCluster(n_workers=2)
client = Client(cluster)

X, y = make_blobs(n_samples=1000, n_features=50, centers=3)

dX = da.from_array(X, chunks=(100, 50))
dy = da.from_array(y, chunks=(100,))

init_scores = dy.map_blocks(
    lambda x: np.repeat(0.8, x.size * 3),
    dtype=np.float64
)

assert init_scores.npartitions == dy.npartitions

dask_model = lgb.DaskLGBMClassifier()
dask_model.fit(dX, dy, init_score=init_scores)

assert dask_model.booster_.trees_to_dataframe()['value'][0] == 0.0
```

There is a chance that this range for the grouped allreduce might be slightly inaccurate in some cases. There is a guarantee that the tensors submitted in a group will be involved in collectives executed on the same cycle, but there is no guarantee that they will all be a part of the same call to `PerformOperation`. For example, if the group contains a set of entries with a combined size that exceeds `HOROVOD_FUSION_THRESHOLD`, that will result in multiple calls to `PerformOperation` on the separate fused buffers. Subsequently, the NVTX range will be ended by whichever of those `PerformOperation` calls contains `entries[tensor.size() - 1]`, which isn't necessarily the last one executed. 

Perhaps there is a way to get around this by having the entries of the group share ownership of a range (maybe store a `shared_ptr` to the range in the tensor table entry), and have the range end on object destruction?

    def check_ignore(self, repository_path, path):
       """
       Check if a path is ignored or not.
       
       Args:
         repository_path: Path to the Git repository

        # in git.py Git, add a new method check_ignore that execute git check-ignore -z <path> and parse the result to return the list of ignored files; changed_files method is a good example of a similar method.

        cmd = ["git", "check-ignore", "-z", path]

        response = {}

            response["message"] = e.output.decode("utf-8")

Hmm... we probably don't want child class to be able to override this one. So I am going to change it into `__` instead of `_`.

This _should be obvious_ but honestly, it is easy to forget how some things work.

TLDR: creating an `__attribute` in reality creates a `_CLS_attribute` where `CLS` is the class name and using `self.__attribute` resolves to `_CLS2_attribute` where `CLS2` is the class in which the method that uses the attribute is defined.

An example below.
```
In [10]: class A:
    ...:     def __init__(self):
    ...:         self.x = self._foo
    ...:         self.y = self.__bar
    ...:     def _foo(self):
    ...:         print("A.foo")
    ...:     def __bar(self):
    ...:         print("A.bar")
    ...:
    ...:

In [11]: class B(A):
    ...:     def _foo(self):
    ...:         print("B.foo")
    ...:     def  __bar(self):
    ...:         print("B.bar")
    ...:

In [12]: B().x()
B.foo

In [13]: B().y()
A.bar
```

> Thoughts on what we should do here?

This brings to mind another potential issue:
1. The checkpoint contains a resource with URN A and a resource with URN B that is a child of the former.
2. A resource with URN C that is aliased to URN A is registered.
3. A resource with URN A is registered.
4. A resource with URN B is registered that is a child of the resource with URN C.

When the checkpoint is written between steps (3) and (4), the new resource with URN A will appear to be the parent of the old resource with URN B.

I believe that similar problems will crop up with dependencies, providers, etc. Those issues would be much more worrisome, as that information is required for correctness. The more I think about it, the more I am convinced that we will either need to fix up URN references in the checkpoint before writing it if any aliases have been used, or we will need to fix consumers of that information to consider aliases when reading the checkpoint. At that point, we would need to bump the checkpoint version number, as the alias information would be required for correctness.

`:manager` invokes whatever `tensorboard(1)` is on the system path.
`:manager` can’t depend on `:tensorboard`, because `:tensorboard`
depends on it! But the test can depend on `:tensorboard` and add that to
the system path so that `:manager` picks up the binary:

```python
    # Add our Bazel-provided `tensorboard` to the system path.
    tensorboard_binary_dir = os.path.realpath("./tensorboard/")
    path_environ = {
        "PATH": os.pathsep.join((tensorboard_binary_dir, os.environ["PATH"])),
    }
    path_environ_patcher = mock.patch.dict(os.environ, path_environ)
    path_environ_patcher.start()
```

It doesn’t directly _invoke_ it, but it does directly _consume_ it (by
adding it to the path as listed above).

A while after I added the comment, I actually also thought of the same model as you suggested 😃 (i.e., if we can add a `otDnsBrowseResponseGetNextTxtEntry` for `BrowseResponse` itself using `TxtRecord::GetNextEntry()`)

I don't think that it will be straight-forward:
- The `GetNextEntry()` requires `aTxtData` array to be given to it so it can parse through the entries. 
- In the case of `otDnsBrowseResponse`, we won't have the txt data in an array but we will have it in the response `Message`. 
- I don't see any easy way to extend `GetNextEntry()` to work with `Message` directly (since we use pointers to `aTxtData` array to get the "key" and "value" segment - which is a good model in my opinion).

The way I was/am considering is that we add a new common `ot` public function (in `dns.h`) like `otDnsGetNextTxtEntry(otDnsTxtEntry *aIterator, const char *aTxtData, uint16_t aTxtDataLength)` to parse any given txt data. This then makes it the caller's job to provide the txt data buffer and its content. The other upside I see is that the same `ot` API can be used for different cases (e.g., browse response or service response) and even maybe for SRP server services.

Hi Abtink
The 'C: TLV flags fields' is full. The 6th and 7th bits are used for storing default router preference data
('high', 'med', or 'low' status). I have added a new 'C: TLV flags fields extended' to keep additional flags separatelly.
The flags field has been added to the end of spinel structure to keep backward compatibility to previous versions.
In the INSERT command I am reading and parsing all the data until first flag field(this are mandatory data that must be present).
Additionally I am checking if "isLocal", 'rloc16' and 'extended flags' fields are present. 
These data is considered as optional.
When the fields "isLocal" and 'rloc16' are present, the variables content is ignored by INSERT command.
If the 'extended flags' data is present, it will be read and processed accordingly.


I have selected mdns flag in the extended flags bajt to be located under 7 bit and Dp flag at 6bit. 
This bits are selected to be compatible with OT kNdDnsFlag and kDpFlag constants provided in the network_data_tlvs.hpp file.

My feeling is that it won't happen. Let me know if I understand it incorrectly:

- run_control_loop will only be called once in init stage
- when serve got updates it'll always go to run_control_loop
- run_control_loop will call deploy (autoscaler) and start to deploy
- your concern here is that:
  - the deploy is async and before it finished, it'll return and run_control_loop will continue to run (sleep and deploy)

If this is true, I don't think it's an issue since although it's async run, it still guarantee the ordering.

For this one: 
```
        return await asyncio.gather(
            *[self.deploy(**args) for args in deployment_args_list]
```

So if inside self.deploy it has some await, the next self.deploy might happen.

If we change it to
```
        return [ await self.deploy(**args) for args in deployment_args_list]
```

The ordering will be: unless the first one finished, the next wont start.


For run_control_loop case, it's like:

1. await autoscaler
2. something
3. ..
4. asyncio.sleep()
5. go to 1)

Here 2 won't be scheduled unless 1) is finished. It's not a detached task, the await will yield and until the coroutine finished, it'll resume from the place where it yield.

For the sake of user convenience, I'm supportive of offering an out-of-the-box implementation of a multi-asset sensor that covers a common pattern, even if we know that there are multi-asset sensing patterns that it does not cover.

I think it's important to make sure the name is scoped to the pattern that we're supporting.  E.g. I'd much prefer a name like `all_assets_updated_sensor` to `multi_asset_sensor`, because it gives us room in the future to add other kinds of multi-asset sensors that support different or wider patterns.

I think that the question of run-level lineage is tricky, and my hunch is that we can offer comparable value with much less headache by sticking to higher-level associations.  I.e. I think there's a decent amount of value in being able to tell users "the sensor for job X cares about materializations of asset Y", and that that would be relatively easy to implement.  I think that trying to tell users "materialization Y caused run X to be launched" is an order of magnitude trickier, especially in the multi-asset case, and not that much more valuable.

You mean `Optional[Union[str, List[str]]]`. Well, technically a `Optional[T]` is a `Union[None, T]` in fact. Most of the time `Optional` is more elegant, but if we are talking about a `Union` type, I find a little more elegant to use only two levels of generics (here `Union` and `List`) instead of three (`Union`, `List`  and `Optional`).

For sure PEP 604 is way more elegant, because we could use `List[str] | str | None`, but it is native to Python 3.10. For the other Python versions, we need `typing_extensions` module and declare `from __future__ import annotations`. I am not that in a hurry to go back to our lovely `from __future__ import absolute_import, print_function` of the era of Python 2->3 migration ^^

Yes, the case is hit any time when we try to export a categorical column from Omnisci having a None in it. A null value is internally represent with the minimal possible value for the array's type, meaning that int32 array of `[1, None, 2, 3]` is actually a `[1, -2147483648, 2, 3]`. The same is true for categorical data that is exported from Omnisci:

<details><summary>Reproducer</summary>

```python
import pandas
import modin.pandas as pd
from modin.core.dataframe.pandas.exchange.dataframe_protocol.from_dataframe import (
    buffer_to_ndarray,
)
from modin.experimental.core.execution.native.implementations.omnisci_on_native.exchange.dataframe_protocol.buffer import (
    OmnisciProtocolBuffer,
)
from modin.core.dataframe.base.exchange.dataframe_protocol.utils import DTypeKind
from modin.experimental.core.execution.native.implementations.omnisci_on_native.test.utils import (
    ForceOmnisciImport,
)


df = pd.DataFrame({"a": pandas.Categorical(["a", None, "b", "c"])})

with ForceOmnisciImport(df) as handler:
    df = handler.export_frames()[0]
    at = df._query_compiler._modin_frame._partitions[0, 0].arrow_table

    bitwidth = at.column(0).chunks[0].indices.type.bit_width
    validity, data = at.column(0).chunks[0].buffers()

    codes = buffer_to_ndarray(
        OmnisciProtocolBuffer(data), dtype=(DTypeKind.INT, bitwidth, None, None)
    )
    print(codes)
```

Output:
```
[          0 -2147483648           1           2]
```

</details>

> because `classmethod` should return `Self`.

That's not even remotely true, a `classmethod` is merely a method that takes the class object itself and uses it in any way. The only reason class methods are often used as alternative constructors is precisely because of this advantage of having the class object itself bound to it's first argument. But this bound class object can be used for anything, not just for initialization of that class, but to for example refer to class constants, other static methods, or even other class methods.

Consider having a child class which inherits from this class. Our child class would then end up inheriting this method directly, without updating the explicit reference to `Motd`. If that class then overridden the `_parse_as_dict` method, when this staticmethod would get called, it would ignore this override and simply refer to the original implementation defined on the `Motd` class. This is an anti-pattern which should be avoided.

Class methods simply provide a clean and pythonic way to always get the proper class object without the need to handle it manually, by for example making a static method which forces the user to pass in the class object manually.

Here Yiddish is acting like a Germanic language (which it is mostly, at least from a morphological point of view). The regular past participle starts with the prefix "ge-", as in "ge-zungen" for the verb "zingen" (to sing).
In the vast majority of cases, it's valid to remove this "ge-". However, there are some common exception, including "gebn" (to give) and "gelt" (money). There are certainly some other exceptions that I could add with time.

However, after the verbal particles, you will never find "gelt" as it isn't a verb.
You could have "avek-gebn" (to give away), but never "avek-gelt".

Also, in prefixed verbs, the "ge-" past participle marker comes after the particle, giving, "avek-ge-gebn" for "gave away". So we only include the "gebn" exception in past participles, not the "gelt".

The form "avek-tsu-gebn" is another particularity (possibly it exists in German as well, but I don't speak any German!). The preposition "tsu" (to) is inserted inside a verb with a particle prefix, e.g. "avek-tsu-gebn" (to give away).

Actually,  you can achieve the same effect using RDFLib as is, using just:
```python
from rdflib import Graph

graph = Graph()
graph.parse('https://prefix.cc/context.jsonld')

for pfx, ns in graph.namespaces():
    print(pfx, ns)
```
This yields one very interesting difference though: the `go` prefix won't work, as it ends in a `_`, which is not [treated as a namespace prefix in JSON-LD 1.1](https://www.w3.org/TR/json-ld/#compact-iris), since it does not en with a [URI gen-delim](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-2.2) (it has to be explicitly declared using `"@prefix": true` in the context to be treated as a prefix anyway).

Looks like th current code assumes that the caller is provding the buffer for `aAdvData` (i.e. the content is not copied an just the pointer is saved).

There are pros/cons for this model (mainly that it makes it more complex for caller/user of OT), but I think it is good and flexible model for the initial implementation.


_Assuming we choose to go for this model (i.e., caller need to provides the buffer/object)_, I suggest we change it as follows:
- We define a new type `otJoinerAdvData` 
```
/**
 * This structure represents a Joiner Advertisement Data.
 *
 */
typedef struct otJoinerAdvData
{
    uint32_t                      mOui;        ///< Vendor OUI
    const uint8_t *               mData;       ///< Pointer to buffer containing the Adv Data
    uint8_t                       mDataLength; ///< The length of data (in bytes)
    const struct otJoinerAdvData *mNext;       ///< For internal OT core use.
} otJoinerAdvData;
```
- We define `otThreadAddJoinerAdvData(otInstance *, const otJoinAdvData *)` and `otThreadRemoveAdvData()` to allow user to add and remove entries (e.g., associated with different OUIs)
- We require the `otJoinerAdvData` instance that the caller provdies to remain valid.
- Within OT core just put the given objects into a linked-list.

The way I’ve written it, subclasses add headers by appending to
`self.headers`. (I figured that this was simplest.) If these references
were shared, then in

```python
e1 = errors.UnauthenticatedError(challenge="one")
e2 = errors.UnauthenticatedError(challenge="two")
```

both `e1` and `e2` would have a `headers` list with two entries (one
with challenge `one`, one with challenge `two`) due to the mutation
pollution.

In general, this is just “avoid shared mutable state”. The test fails if
you write the initialization as

```python
class PublicError(RuntimeError):
    http_code = 400
    headers = []
```

rather than

```python
class PublicError(RuntimeError):
    http_code = 400
    def __init__(...):
        self.headers = []
```

even though the former looks reasonable to an untrained eye.

This code is mostly necessary for testing and slurm deploy. 

For listener to differentiate between connected peers they need a different ID.  It is quite fine that init container and communication container use the same ID, since they are not active at the same moment.

On slurm/testing, the executor starts on a possibly different machine, connects to the listener and bootstraps the docker container.  The executor also sends logs to the listener (how much time it took to start the docker container, report exceptions etc) and can therefore be active at the same time as containers. So it must use a different ID. Before I used `e_{data_id}`, but having minus makes code in the listener nicer.

Not sure if we want to do it in this PR or split this off to a follow-up cc @datumbox. Nevertheless this bothered me a bit to much TBH. I wrote a small `flake8` plugin to find all occurrences of this. Put the following in a `nuakw.py` file in the root of the project

```py
import ast
import contextlib


class NoUnpackingAfterKeywordFinder(ast.NodeVisitor):
    def __init__(self):
        self.errors = []

    def visit_Call(self, node: ast.Call):
        with contextlib.suppress(StopIteration):
            starred = next(starred for starred in node.args if isinstance(starred, ast.Starred))
            if any(
                (keyword.value.lineno, keyword.value.col_offset) < (starred.lineno, starred.col_offset)
                for keyword in node.keywords
            ):
                self.errors.append(
                    (node.lineno, node.col_offset, "NUAKW Argument unpacking after keyword argument", type(self))
                )

        self.generic_visit(node)


class NoUnpackingAfterKeyword:
    name = "no_unpacking_after_keyword"
    version = "0.0.1"

    def __init__(self, tree: ast.AST):
        self._tree = tree

    def run(self):
        visitor = NoUnpackingAfterKeywordFinder()
        visitor.visit(self._tree)
        yield from visitor.errors

```

Now, put the following at the bottom of `setup.cfg`

```
[flake8:local-plugins]
extension =
    NUAKW = nuakw:NoUnpackingAfterKeyword
paths =
    ./

```

The equivalent case for `go` would be if multiple packages had cyclic imports for one another: but it looks like that  isn't possible: https://jogendra.dev/import-cycles-in-golang-and-how-to-deal-with-them

I added this commit: a version bump for Python 3.12+ (I think of it as a free lunch version bump):

```diff
 #ifndef PYBIND11_INTERNALS_VERSION
-#    define PYBIND11_INTERNALS_VERSION 4
+#    if PY_VERSION_HEX >= 0x030C0000
+// Version bump for Python 3.12+, before first 3.12 beta release.
+#        define PYBIND11_INTERNALS_VERSION 5
+#    else
+#        define PYBIND11_INTERNALS_VERSION 4
+#    endif
 #endif
```

Then I looked long and hard at the condition here but eventually decided to leave it at a no-op shuffle, solely to increase readability slightly (see e8b34b473546c469f11cd2c2a0cb098ca12134e5).

> it might be simpler

Did you have in mind removing the `PY_VERSION_HEX < 0x030C0000` sub-condition here?

What worries me: if someone defines `PYBIND11_INTERNALS_VERSION 4` externally, we'd select the wrong code here if the sub-condition is missing. We don't know yet what the resulting errors might be, if any, for future Python releases.

I guess we could do something like this to guard the `PyEval_GetBuiltins()` path:

```
#if PY_VERSION_HEX >= 0x030C0000
#error Python 3.12+ requires PYBIND11_INTERNALS_VERSION >= 5
#endif
```

But that's strictly speaking an arbitrary forcing function. Maybe someone will have a good reason to experiment with `PYBIND11_INTERNALS_VERSION` 4 even for 3.12+? E.g. when debugging some issue with the TLS-related changes connected to version 5. This is totally made up of course, but I'm generally trying to stay clear of non-essential forcing functions (anecdotes of getting burned omitted).

Using reshape on the right hand side of the assignment makes an unnecessary copy because it must convert the RHS to a numpy array:
```
>>> import numpy
>>> x=[1,2,3,4]
>>> y = numpy.reshape(x, (2,2))
>>> y
array([[1, 2],
       [3, 4]])
>>> x[0]=10
>>> y
array([[1, 2],
       [3, 4]])
```

The left hand side of the assignment is already a numpy array, so if you use reshape on the LHS then it will create a view object for the existing array and it will not make a copy.
```
>>> import numpy
>>> x = numpy.array([[1,2],[3,4]])
>>> x
array([[1, 2],
       [3, 4]])
>>> numpy.reshape(x, (4,))[:] = [5,6,7,8]
>>> x
array([[5, 6],
       [7, 8]])
```

Watch out: it appears that the RC worker is operating in a thread.

Upon forking, typically all threads except the one that called `fork` are killed in the child. This means that if the thread is killed in the middle of updating the client state when processing an RC update the client state will be inconsistent and cannot be relied upon.

In that case generating a new client id and reusing the client internal state is insufficient. For Ruby we are going to simply create a new client instance (which makes sense since it is a new client): a clean state and new client id will be generated RAII style.

So just to understand the code, there are 2 situations in which platformRadioTxDone() can be called:

1. Rx node completed sending an Ack (sState = Rx , sLastReportedState = Tx)
2. Tx node completed sending a non-ack (data) frame  (sState = Tx , sLastReportedState = Tx)

In case 1 it will hit the top 'if' part of the code, since sAckTxDonePending was true. The transmitted Ack frame data is sitting in the sAckFrame buffer. The sTransmitFrame buffer holds unrelated data that shouldn't be touched.

In case 2 it will hit the bottom  'else if' part of the code. In this case the data frame transmitted sits in the sTransmitFrame buffer so the code may refer to it:

```

Oh sorry if my help string wasn't clear! One needs an installation prefix for a Debian package to specify where the Debian package will be installed on the system (once the packaged is produced). This information determines the directory hierarchy within the package. 

```
├── example-pex
│   ├── DEBIAN
│   │   └── control
│   ├── opt
│   │   └── company
│   │       └── example
│   │           └── example-linux-cp3.6.pex
│   └── usr
│       └── bin
│           └── do-work -> /opt/company/example/example-linux-cp3.6.pex
```

Here the user has specified /opt/company/example as installation prefix and thus a Debian package with these directory tree is produced. When user will install the Debian package, the files will end up under their parent directories on the file system (so the `example-linux-cp3.6.pex` will end up under `/opt/company/example` directory.

So Pants of course will not install the Debian package -- the installation prefix is needed solely to produce a Debian with the right directory hierarchy. Hope this makes sense now?

> For these reasons, it’s encouraged to list copyright and license data explicitly at the top of each file.

We don't feel that we'll actually encourage this and retain this as a convention. We would like to support this situation, but without additional hurdles and conventions. So I would like to request some rephrasing here.

There seem to be 2 primary situations:
- vendored code snippets from another already licensed project
- new code where the creators desire this description

For vendored code snippets, it's absolutely appropriate to include copyright and licensing declarations within the file or alongside the code that was copied. We could, and probably should, mention that here.

For new code, and perhaps even whole complete vendored files, I suggest that we take a centralized metadata approach that the REUSE specification mentions, by having a file `.reuse/dep5` which itemizes each of these declarations.
```
Format: https://www.debian.org/doc/packaging-manuals/copyright-format/1.0/
Upstream-Name: kolibri
Upstream-Contact: Learning Equality <info@learningequality.org>
Source: https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri

Files: path/to/file
Copyright: 2023 Ann Contributor
License: MIT
```

When use internal redis, we start primary instance in the head node and sharding instances per `shard ports` specified, which means we always have sharding.
But with external redis, we allow only one remote redis provided, that means we must reuse the primary as a shard. We can always reuses it in external redis supporting. Even it is little different from internal one, at least it is consistent with external no matter the number of redis instances is 1 or more.

1. Only store relative logdirs in e.g. `__checkpoint_relative_dirs` or so instead of the whole state. Then only override this on restore
2. Store the full state here, but do not store the state in the checkpoint manager

```
# getstate
checkpoint_manager_state = state["checkpoint_manager"]
checkpoints = checkpoint_manager_state.pop("_top_persisted_checkpoints")
for checkpoint in checkpoints:
    # ...
state["__checkpoint_states"] = checkpoint_states

# setstate
checkpoint_manager_state = state["checkpoint_manager"]
for checkpoint in state.pop("__checkpoint_states", []):
    top_persisted_checkpoints = ...
checkpoint_manager_state["_top_persisted_checkpoints"] = top_persisted_checkpoints
```

Awkward's `simplify_uniontype` would turn a union of integers and floating point numbers into an array of floating point numbers, following NumPy's type promotion rules, and maybe that would get used on this someday.

When I manually checked the sample code in `README.rst`, mypy complained that a mandatory `BaseObserver.__init__()` argument was not being filled. I couldn't find a way to show that *all* of the `BaseObserver` child classes override `.__init__()`, but found a way to convince mypy *enough* of the child classes do by telling mypy to only pay attention to the imports that matched the platform mypy is running on.

To do that, I replaced the `.is_<platform>()` calls with code that mypy understood. mypy supports the `sys.platform == ...` and `sys.platform.startswith(...)` syntax for marking code as platform-specific, and doing so here told mypy to skip checking ALL possible imports of `Observer` and instead only focus on the `Observer` imports that it actually could import.

When running on Windows, for example, mypy stopped caring about the `KqueueObserver` (which it couldn't import and introspect), which alleviated the complaint about the code in `README.rst`.

Hopefully that explanation makes sense! It's the only way I found to get the code in `README.rst` to pass so #840 can be closed.

The callbacks will be triggered whenever the event arrives. E.g. when the future resolves, the `on_result` callback will be called. When the future errors, the `on_error` callback will be called.

For this we have to yield control to the event manager via `manager.wait()`. The alternative would be to do this in a separate thread, but then we run into problems with thread-safety, so I think for now that's the best way to go forward.

> * What is the use case for a walkthrough over all "things" without having to instantiate the instruments (and also, by extension, the things themselves)? Why is it important that we are able to do this?

I was thinking about the possibility of having a class method that walk through the instrument definition and produce some documentation or tree like representation of the instrument features implemented.

> * Why is it better to have channels defined at class level? Where is the distinction important? Actually, only the channel "schema"/makeup (if you will) is defined at class level, as you say the channels themselves are created at instantiation.

I think features related to instrument are more appropriately implemented at class level, since they do not change with a particular instance.

> * Why is it easier to reuse channels in different instruments? The channels are separately defined classes, irrespective of which scheme we are using to put them into the instrument, no?

Absolutely correct. Here my point was that copying a class attribute definition is easier than inspecting the code to see how the class was initialized and used.

> 
> I also want to raise some advantages of the currently implemented method to cover point "3" above, over the approach you propose:
> 
> * It is easier, via `add_child`'s `prefix` argument with a sensible default, to make clear and encourage/enforce the default naming scheme (i.e. `ch_<id1>`, `ch_<id2>`,...) for the attributes. This will automatically lead to a more homogeneous API across instruments (which we want to see more of in the future, see also [Instrument's classes #416](https://github.com/pymeasure/pymeasure/issues/416)). If instrument implementors need to define this in a "things" dict, where they have choose their own keys, this will be less automatic, and I suspect it will need more effort during PR review to correct.

Here, I am sorry I see little value in defining the default name `ch`, I mean it does not harm, but really the name should be derived from what is written on the instrument documentation or front panel.

> * I'm not clear where in the "things" mapping we supply the identifiers of the channel (be it `1`, `2`, or `A`, `B`,...) in the exchanged command strings. This is currently handled in `add_child`'s `id` argument, how do you map the things dict to that? Use the keys?

Indeed, this is the part that I mentioned in my proposal "I acknowledge that some implementation details (which I hope are minor) are neglected in my explanation".

As a second thought, it is probably best to mimic what is done for commands, i.e static method that defines properties, for example:

```
class MyInstrument4ChannelModel(Instrument):
    analog_input = Instrument.channel(MyAnalogInput, ...) # parameters should be similar to add_child
.
.
.

```

> * We avoid a class attribute `things` that clutters the already complicated Instrument API, reserves another name, but is useful only during instantiation. Granted, we could delete that in `Instrument.**init**, but still.

Honestly, I do not see this as a problem, I could argue that adding a public API `add_child` is taking one name and user cannot use it!

> 
> In the end this will be a trade-off between different points. Happy to discuss this further to get to a well-rounded design! :-)

Yeah, I get that. Unfortunately, MARC relator codes are actually one of the few "standards" for a common vocabulary relating a person to a document, and are probably (by far) the most used. As I think I mentioned earlier, the MARC relator codes are the ones endorsed by Dublin Core itself.

Other standard possibilities include the Getty vocabularies, but they're even more esoteric and harder to figure out how to apply. Or the RDA Vocabularies but .... well, here's an example: http://www.rdaregistry.info/Elements/w/#P10053

Not something I would necessarily foist on an unsuspecting public. :-D

Googling "MARC Relator Codes" gives me about 540,000 hits. (They were invented far before prefixes were 'normal' so using 'marcrel' or some short form isn't likely to find much.)

As I mentioned, if you've got the full URI for the codes stored somewhere, then the likelihood that you're not able to find a reference to them is low in the future; also, it's highly unlikely that they will change anytime soon -- that would necessitate a vast amount of work in library catalogue systems!
